text: The surgeon begins the procedure by exploring and localizing the lesion at the distal transverse colon, confirmed by visual examination and ink stain. The transverse colon is lifted to expose the ligament of Treitz, followed by an incision on the mesocolon above the ligament. Dissection proceeds cranially above the pancreas and along the lateral side of the inferior mesenteric vein, where vascular branches to the left colon are carefully dissected, clipped, and divided. The dissection continues along the tissue plane anterior to the Toldt fascia, which protects the ureter and ovarian vessels, with the pancreatic tail visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects up to the apex, reaching the parietal peritoneum of the splenocolic ligament, revealing the spleen upon incision. Attention is then redirected to the transverse mesocolon, where dissection above the pancreas enters the lesser sac cavity, encountering the stomach. The dissection continues medially and laterally along the pancreas to detach the mesocolon.

The surgeon divides the transverse mesocolon toward the proximal line of resection, approximately five to six centimeters from the lesion, and divides the arterial branch from the middle colic artery. The surrounding fat and omentum are cleared, and the transverse colon is transected using endo-GI staplers. Attention is then turned to the distal colon, where the upper third of the descending colon is dissected until circumferentially free and transected with endo-GI staplers. The specimen is detached from the renocolic and splenocolic ligaments, and the gastrocolic ligament is divided in the lesser sac, leaving most of it on the specimen.

The surgeon divides the remaining splenocolic ligament to complete the resection. The resected specimen is placed into a specimen bag. The proximal transverse colon, hepatic flexure, and ascending colon are mobilized, and the surgeon confirms there is no tension before beginning the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal hand-sewn anastomosis of the colon. The posterior seromuscular layer is sutured using a running 3-0 silk suture, followed by the remaining layers using 3-0 V-lock sutures, which are braided absorbable sutures that lock onto the tissue.
